title: Conditional Formatting
summary: Conditional Formatting automatically colours, highlights, or adds icons to cells based on rules you set, so important values stand out without manual styling.
syntax: Select range > Home > Conditional Formatting > choose a rule type > set condition and format
jobUseCases: Highlight overdue invoices in red on an accounts-receivable tracker
Flag KPIs below target with traffic-light icons on a sales dashboard
Shade duplicate employee IDs to catch data-entry errors in an HR sheet
Use a colour scale to show heat across a regional performance grid
Highlight the entire row of any high-priority support ticket

examples.title: Highlight values greater than a target
examples.formula: Select Sales > Home > Conditional Formatting > Highlight Cells Rules > Greater Than > 50000 > Green Fill
examples.explanation: Select the Sales column, then apply Greater Than with 50000. Every cell above 50,000 gets a green fill automatically, instantly showing which deals beat the target.

examples.title: Find and shade duplicates
examples.formula: Select range > Conditional Formatting > Highlight Cells Rules > Duplicate Values
examples.explanation: Choose Duplicate Values on a list of employee IDs or emails. Excel highlights any value that appears more than once, making it easy to spot and clean up data-quality issues like a duplicated 'Priya Sharma' record.

examples.title: Data bars for quick comparison
examples.formula: Select numbers > Conditional Formatting > Data Bars > Gradient Fill
examples.explanation: Data Bars draw a small in-cell bar proportional to each value, so a column of monthly revenue reads like a mini bar chart. Larger numbers get longer bars, letting readers compare magnitudes at a glance.

examples.title: Formula-based rule to highlight a whole row
examples.formula: =$E2="Overdue"  (New Rule > Use a formula to determine which cells to format)
examples.explanation: Select the full data range, choose Use a formula, and enter =$E2="Overdue". The locked column ($E) and relative row (2) make Excel format every row whose Status column equals Overdue, highlighting the entire record, not just one cell.

commonMistakes: Using a relative reference like E2 when a formula rule needs the column locked ($E2) to highlight whole rows correctly
Applying the rule to a single cell instead of the full intended range, so most data stays unformatted
Stacking many overlapping rules and not setting the priority/Stop If True order, leading to unexpected colours
Comparing text and numbers (e.g. '50000' stored as text vs the number 50000) so the rule never triggers
Forgetting that conditional formats are copied along with cells, accidentally spreading rules where they do not belong
practiceTips: Use Manage Rules (Conditional Formatting > Manage Rules) to view, edit, and reorder every rule on a sheet.
Reference a cell for thresholds (e.g. >$H$1) so you can change the target value without editing the rule.
Test a formula rule on the top-left cell of your selection first, since that is where Excel anchors it.
Use Clear Rules from Selected Cells to remove stray formatting before reapplying clean rules.
